Sure. I mainly base my tests on unittest because it integrates well with the object-oriented structure of my packages. Additionally, since it is a built-in library, it is very stable and keeps my dependencies for running the tests as low. The approach I would use is have `set_value` replaced with `update_value`, which should be called when an iOS client wants the value to change. Then the callback is responsible for trying to make that happen, and raises and exception if it can't.

For the `Accessory` object, I think that a `@property` setter could be used on the `value` attribute, that triggers the notification process.

I think it's very odd for people to be using this API to have the callback triggered. Presumably anything that wants to manipulate the `Characteristic` will do it via the `Accessory` object. In which case the `Accessory` object would be responsible for calling the callback, or only setting `value` once the callback has completed successfully.

That way there is a clear separation of responsibilities, `Characteristic`s only deal with what needs to be presented to iOS. Marshalling `value` sets to iOS clients as notifications, and marshalling requests from iOS to the `Accessory`. The `Accessory` is then responsible for making sure that the `value` stores in a `Characteristic` matches the real world device, regardless how that value was set.

This would also closely match how JS-HAP works as well, and prevents py-HAP from being tangled into peoples accessory code.

I also support the RTFM / RTFS and force users pass the expected args. Unfortunately, I also don't like when my python scripts crash with no apparent reason and when I stumbled upon those weird domains, it made tldextract crash. 

Problem is a bit more "complex" because a domain that starts with xn-<blabla> can be either a punycode or just .. .a normal domain! Punycode domains are"valid" as long as users want them to be "valid". From this lib's point of view, we don't care if a punycode subdomain is valid or not, we just need to split them. But here comes the problem... in order to try to match a punycode domain from PSL we need to... convert it if possible. At least we need to try. And if we fail.. then bad luck, it wasn't punycode :) But our job is still to split that URL.Hence the need to try/catch conversion. 

Just wanted to clarify what is my reasoning in case there will be future problems :)

Can you explain how retries are handled? For example, we start adding data to the heap and that bumps up the ``self._next_offset``. Then let's say that the download got retried and we start at the beginning again with an offset less than the ``self._next_offset``. It looks like the queue will never be cleared because the reset offset will be smaller than the expected offse and the while loop will never be entered. To fix this, I think you need to check if the given offset is less than the _next_offset and discard it if so.

Note it would be much better if you did the `self.store_global_scope(...)` inside `addBinding`

What happens if there's more than one type of content unit in this repository? For example:

```python
>>> mytext = (
...   'foo bar biz baz\n'
...   'Puppet Module: 5\n'
...   'one two three four'
... )
>>> mytext.split('Puppet Module:')
['foo bar biz baz\n', ' 5\none two three four']
```

In this case, the call to `int(output[1].strip())` will fail. A more robust solution is to find the one line of text containing the keyword 'Puppet Module:', and to parse that one line. Also, this logic seems like something that other test cases might want to make use of. A standalone function will be more re-usable.

Use a function like the following instead:

```python
def _get_n_puppet_modules(server_config, repo_id):
    """Tell how many puppet modules are in a repository.

    :param pulp_smash.config.ServerConfig server_config: Information about the
        Pulp server being targeted.
    :param repo_id: A Puppet repository ID.
    :returns: The number of puppet modules in a repository, as an ``int``.
    """
    keyword = 'Puppet Module:'
    completed_proc = cli.Client(server_config).run((
        'pulp-admin puppet repo list --repo-id {} --fields content_unit_counts'
    ).format(repo_id).split())
    lines = [
        line for line in completed_proc.stdout.splitlines() if keyword in line
    ]
    assert len(lines) < 2
    if len(lines) == 0:
        return 0
    else:
        return int(lines[1].split(keyword)[1].strip())
```

This needs to be in the state tree somewhere, we can't have module-level singletons. Reasons:

* For codebase style, this goes against our flux principles
* For testing, you can't assume that the module is in the same state. Every single function in here now has access to these variables as part of the module closure.
* For modularity reasons -- you can't use this module now to publish multiple gists with one single page app; every single notebook document would have the same gist ID and gist URL

As for what to do, rely on the state tree to set these, even if they're transient data (not in the notebook tree). What was the problem with the metadata field inside the notebook? It's also the one used with @minrk's gist extension.

Thanks for the feedback :) I agree this could be changed to `return sub(r'.*sync_token=', '', self.next_sync_url or self.next_page_url)` but that seems a bit brittle. It assumes that the URL will *always* end with `sync_token=<the_sync_token>`. What happens if the API changes and the value of `nextSyncUrl` or `nextPageUrl` is a URL with multiple query string parameters not ending in `sync_token=MKpLD7ChcKHd...`?

For example, the API returns something like`  {"nextPageUrl": "https://cdn.contentful.com/spaces/o46ru064noqc/sync?sync_token=wonDrcKnRgcSOF4&timestamp=1498558724"}`. Using the regex substitution above would break whereas using urllib to extract the correct parameter makes it resilient to these kinds of changes for very little effort and avoids a potential bug in the future.

An unfortunate quirk of Python is that this loses the original stack trace (unlike in Java, in Python errors do not retain a stack trace, and rather the `raise` mechanism takes the current stack trace).

If you want to retain the stack trace, you should store in `self._read_exception` not the `Exception` object but the 3-tuple result of `sys.exc_info`. Then, if you only care about Python 2, here instead of `raise self._read_exception` write `(type, value, traceback) = self._read_exception; raise type, value, traceback`. [Docs.](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/simple_stmts.html#the-raise-statement) Since we want to be hybrid, instead write `six.reraise(*self._read_exception)`. [Docs.](https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.reraise) (Caveat scriptor: I've not used `six.reraise` before, but it seems like the right thing.)

If you think that this is too much trouble, that's your call.

(This likewise above with `raise self._close_exception`.)

By the way, thanks for being careful about raising exceptions in the right threads. That's a good thing to have.

- I pip install letsencrypt on Python 2.6, pip discovers a valid wheel and installs it, using `install_requires` computed earlier for a different Python version.
